1: Chapter 36 opens with an oracle "to the mountains of Israel" — the direct counterpart to the oracle against the mountains in Ezekiel 6. Where Ezekiel 6 announced desolation for the mountains that hosted the idol-worship, Ezekiel 36 announces restoration for those same mountains now shamed by enemy occupation. The structural pairing of Ezekiel 6 and 36 is one of the major literary symmetries of the book: the same geographical entity that received the judgment oracle now receives the restoration oracle.
2: The enemy's taunt — "Aha! The ancient heights have become our possession" — is the specific insult addressed in the restoration oracle. The "ancient heights" (bamot ʿolam) refers to the highlands of Israel, the geographic heart of the land. The nations who said "Aha!" over Israel's desolation are the same nations condemned in Ezekiel 25 (Ammon, Ezekiel 25:3). The restoration is in part a vindication against these taunters.
3: Being made desolate and pursued from every side so that the nations talked about Israel — the diaspora experience of national humiliation is the context for the restoration promise. The Babylonian exile produced a community perceived by surrounding nations as abandoned by its God. The nations' theological conclusion (that YHWH had failed or been defeated) is precisely the "profaning of YHWH's holy name" that drives the restoration in vv22-23.
4: The address to the mountains, hills, ravines, valleys, desolate wastes, and forsaken cities — the comprehensive geographic inventory of the land as the addressee mirrors the comprehensive destruction oracle of Ezekiel 6. The mountains of Israel are promised that they will bear fruit for YHWH's people Israel; the land itself is a participant in the covenant, capable of responding to YHWH's word.
5: YHWH's jealous fire against the rest of the nations (Edom specifically and all the nations) who took Israel's land with "wholehearted joy and utter contempt." Edom is singled out (Ezekiel 25:12-14; Ezekiel 35:1-15; Obad 1) as the paradigm of the nations who exploited Israel's collapse. Edomite expansion into the Negev following 586 BCE is documented archaeologically in the Negev Highland survey data and in later references to "Idumea" as formerly Edomite territory.
6: YHWH speaking in jealous wrath against the nations who have become a reproach to Israel — the same jealousy (qinʾah) that drove the judgment against Israel (ch Ezekiel 5:13) is now directed against the taunting nations. The divine jealousy is not petulant but covenantal: YHWH's reputation is bound to Israel's story; when Israel is humiliated, YHWH's name is dishonored among the nations.
7: YHWH lifting his hand in oath — the divine oath-gesture (nasati ʾet-yadi, I have raised my hand) is the ANE legal swearing-formula. Raising the hand was the standard gesture for oath-taking in the ancient Near East, documented in both Egyptian and Mesopotamian iconographic sources. YHWH's sworn oath ensures that the nations surrounding Israel will themselves bear shame.
8: "But you, mountains of Israel, shoot your branches and yield your fruit to my people Israel" — the botanical restoration imagery (branches, fruit, agricultural abundance) connects to the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deuteronomy 28:1-14). The promise of fruitfulness is the reversal of the covenant curse of barrenness (Leviticus 26:20; Deuteronomy 28:18). The timing — "for they are coming soon" — gives the restoration an eschatological urgency.
9: "I am for you" — the divine hostile-presence formula inverted: hinneni ʾeleikhem (behold I am for you/toward you), instead of the judgment formula hinneni ʾaleikha (I am against you). The agricultural activity (turning toward them, being cultivated, being sown) personifies the mountains as a farming operation under divine management. The plowing, sowing, and population-increase are the agricultural covenant-blessing triad.
10: Multiplying people on the mountains of Israel — all the house of Israel, all of it — the explicit inclusion of "all of it" anticipates the reunification of Ephraim and Judah announced in Ezekiel 37. The cities rebuilt and the waste places inhabited reverse the destruction oracle of ch Ezekiel 6:6 ("cities shall be laid waste and the high places be desolate"). The architectural and demographic restoration are inseparable.
11: The multiplication of people and animals on the mountains — a prosperity formula drawn from the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deuteronomy 28:11, "YHWH will make you abound in prosperity in the fruit of your womb and in the fruit of your livestock and in the fruit of your ground"). The declaration "I will settle you as in your former times and will do more good to you than ever before" is YHWH's self-commitment to exceed even the pre-exilic prosperity of the Solomonic period.
12: "I will let people walk on you, even my people Israel" — the land is addressed as the place where human beings walk. The land will no longer bereave Israel of children — an unusual promise suggesting that the land itself had been "consuming" its inhabitants through the wars and deportations. This personification of the land as a devourer that YHWH now restrains addresses the community's fear that the land itself had become hostile to human habitation.
13: The nations' taunt that the land "devours people and bereaves its nation of children" — a specific accusation the surrounding nations had made about the land of Canaan. The land's reputation as a devourer of its inhabitants appears already in the pre-settlement spy report (Numbers 13:32, "a land that devours its inhabitants"). YHWH's promise reverses this reputation by ending the land's consuming character.
14: "You shall no longer devour people and no longer bereave your nation of children" — the divine promise addressed to the land itself. The grammatical second-person address to the land throughout Ezekiel 36 is not merely personification but a genuine oracle directed at the created order: the land has agency in the covenant relationship, capable of receiving divine promises alongside the people.
15: The ending of the nations' taunts and the end of the peoples' insults — the social humiliation of exile addressed alongside the agricultural restoration. The land will no longer cause the nation to stumble (the exile had caused many to conclude that YHWH had failed); YHWH declares that the land's covenant relationship with its people is restored.
16: A new oracle unit addresses the reason for the exile. The word-event formula resets the prophetic speech. The retrospective analysis of the exile's cause (vv16-21) is the theological foundation for the restoration's motivation (vv22-38).
17: The house of Israel living on their land defiling it "like the defilement of a menstruant woman" — the impurity language (ketumat niddah) applies the strongest Levitical purity category to the covenant violation. The land itself had become ritually unclean through Israel's bloodshed and idol-worship, as if defiled by the most serious form of bodily impurity. The Levitical connection between land-defilement and human moral violation (Leviticus 18:24-28; Numbers 35:33-34) is the theological principle at work.
18: YHWH pouring out his fury on them for the blood they shed in the land and for the idols with which they defiled it — the two-charge formula (blood + idols) that has been the structural basis of Ezekiel's indictment throughout (cf. Ezekiel 22:3-4). The fury poured out corresponds to the cup of wrath imagery of Ezekiel 23 and the metallurgical furnace of Ezekiel 22.
19: The scattering among the nations according to their ways and deeds — the exile as lex talionis. The pattern of Israel's social life was returned to them as the shape of their historical experience. The specific description "according to their ways and according to their deeds I judged them" establishes that the exile was not arbitrary but proportionate to the specific patterns of covenant violation documented in Ezekiel 1-24.
20: "When they came to the nations, wherever they came, they profaned my holy name, in that people said of them, 'These are the people of YHWH, and yet they had to go out of his land.'" — the theological crisis of the exile: the surrounding nations drew the conclusion that YHWH had failed or been defeated. This theological embarrassment — YHWH's reputation among the nations being damaged by Israel's exile — is the specific concern that drives the restoration. YHWH's honor among the nations is at stake.
21: "But I had concern for my holy name, which the house of Israel had profaned among the nations" — the motivation for restoration is explicitly NOT Israel's repentance or merit but YHWH's own reputation (shem qadshi, holy name). This is the most theologically striking move in the book: the restoration is grounded not in Israel's worthiness but in YHWH's self-interest in his own honor among the nations. The Deuteronomy 9:4-5 pattern ("not because of your righteousness") is the background framework.
22: "Therefore say to the house of Israel: Thus says Lord YHWH: It is not for your sake that I am about to act, O house of Israel, but for the sake of my holy name." — this explicit denial of Israel's merit as the basis for restoration is theologically radical. ANE restoration theologies typically portrayed the people's repentance or a king's intercession as what moved the deity to restore. Ezekiel's oracle grounds restoration entirely in divine initiative and self-interest, preparing for the new-heart promise that follows: the repentance will be the result, not the cause, of restoration.
23: YHWH vindicating the holiness of his name among the nations — the mission-statement of the restoration. The nations will know that YHWH is YHWH when he is vindicated through Israel. The recognition formula here functions at the universal (national) level: the restoration of Israel is an event that communicates YHWH's identity to all nations. This is the Isaianic "servant of YHWH" paradigm (Isaiah 49:6, "a light to the nations") applied to the restored community.
24: The gathering from the nations and bringing into the land — the standard gathering-from-diaspora promise (also Ezekiel 11:17; Ezekiel 20:34, 41-42; Ezekiel 34:13; Ezekiel 37:21). The sequence — first gathering, then cleansing, then new heart — establishes that YHWH acts first; the community's transformation follows the divine action. This is the opposite of a conditional promise where Israel must first change and then YHWH will gather them.
25: "I will sprinkle clean water on you and you will be clean; from all your uncleannesses and from all your idols I will cleanse you." — the ritual cleansing by sprinkling (zaraqti ʿaleyhem mayim tehorim) invokes the purification ritual of Numbers 19 (the water of purification made from the ashes of the red heifer, used to cleanse corpse-defilement). The application of this ritual to the entire community (not just individuals with specific contamination) is an unprecedented use of the purification-water concept.
26: "A new heart I will give you, and a new spirit I will put within you; I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh." — the climax of Ezekiel's theology of restoration. The new heart (lev ḥadash) and new spirit (ruach ḥadashah) address the root cause of Israel's failure: not external circumstances but the internal orientation of the community. The heart of stone (lev haʾeven) vs. heart of flesh (lev basar) contrast is the most powerful diagnosis of the human condition in the prophetic literature — the inability to respond to YHWH's word is built into the community's psychological structure. The anticipation of this promise in 11:19 and its parallel in Jeremiah 31:31-34 (the new covenant) establish the new heart/new spirit as the central promise of the post-exilic restoration theology.
27: "And I will put my Spirit within you, and cause you to walk in my statutes and be careful to obey my rules." — the divine Spirit (YHWH's own ruach) as the internal enabler of covenant obedience. The Spirit does not merely inspire but causes (ʿasiti, qal perfect of ʿasah, but the causative nuance is important) the obedience — obedience becomes the effect of the Spirit's indwelling rather than a prerequisite for divine favor. This sequence (Spirit first, then obedience) is the theological foundation of Paul's argument in Romans 8:1-4.
28: Dwelling in the land they were given — the covenantal land-dwelling formula. The covenant declaration follows: "you shall be my people and I shall be your God" — the shortest and most foundational form of the Sinaitic covenant (Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 31:33). The covenant formula functions as the summary statement that the restoration fulfills the original covenantal purpose of the Exodus.
29: Salvation from uncleannesses — calling for the grain and multiplying it. The agricultural abundance that follows the internal cleansing and covenant restoration is the Deuteronomic blessing vocabulary (Deuteronomy 28:1-14) now grounded in internal transformation rather than external compliance. The agricultural economy of the ancient Near East made harvest abundance the most tangible sign of divine favor; the restoration of grain, wine, and oil (v30) restores the economic foundation of covenant life.
30: Multiplying the fruit of trees and produce of the field — the threefold agricultural formula (grain + fruit + harvest) is the Deuteronomic blessing triad (Deuteronomy 28:11). The removal of the famine-reproach among the nations is the social vindication that parallels the divine-name vindication of vv22-23: just as YHWH's name is vindicated among the nations by the restoration, the community is vindicated against the famine-reproach that the nations had associated with Israel during the siege and exile.
31: "Then you will remember your evil ways and your deeds that were not good, and you will loathe yourselves for your iniquities and your abominations." — the sequence is confirmed: restoration first, then repentance. The community will remember and loathe themselves after the restoration has occurred, not before. This post-restoration self-loathing (also Ezekiel 20:43; Ezekiel 16:63) is the reversed sequence that makes Ezekiel's theology of grace distinctive: YHWH restores the undeserving, and they respond with shame rather than pride.
32: "It is not for your sake that I will act" — the explicit anti-merit declaration is repeated from v22. The double declaration (vv22 and 32) creates a literary frame around the restoration promise, ensuring the reader does not misread the restoration as conditional on Israel's merit. The shame and disgrace Israel bears is the appropriate response to undeserved grace rather than a prerequisite that earns it.
33: "On the day I cleanse you from all your iniquities, I will cause the cities to be inhabited and the waste places shall be rebuilt" — the synchronization of inner cleansing and external reconstruction. The same day that the new heart is given, the physical land is rebuilt. The inner and outer restoration happen together rather than sequentially, because the land itself participates in the covenant relationship.
34: The desolate land tilled instead of being the desolation in the sight of all who passed by — the land's reputation among travelers is restored. Ancient trade routes ran through Canaan; the desolate landscape left by the Babylonian conquest was visible to travelers and traders who passed through, contributing to the international perception of YHWH's failure. The restored land's cultivation reverses this international witness.
35: "They will say: This desolate land has become like the garden of Eden" — the Eden comparison is the highest possible standard for land-quality. The garden of Eden as the ideal of abundant, well-watered, flourishing land is the eschatological goal of restoration. The ruined and forsaken and desolate cities made fortified and inhabited — the urban reconstruction is the human correlate of the agricultural restoration.
36: The nations round about knowing that YHWH has rebuilt the ruined places and replanted the desolated land — the restoration is a public, internationally visible event. The recognition formula is applied to the surrounding nations who observe the restoration. The witnessing nations' acknowledgment of YHWH's action in restoring Israel completes the "for my name's sake" motivation of v22-23.
37: "I, YHWH, have spoken and I will do it" — the divine self-commitment to accomplish what has been declared. This formula occurs throughout Ezekiel's restoration oracles (Ezekiel 22:14; Ezekiel 24:14; Ezekiel 37:14) as the absolute guarantee that the prophetic word will be fulfilled. Unlike human speech that can be retracted, YHWH's spoken word carries the force of accomplished fact from the moment it is uttered.
38: YHWH responding to Israel's inquiry by multiplying people like a flock, like the flock of Jerusalem at her appointed feasts — the pilgrimage-flock analogy. Jerusalem's three annual pilgrimage feasts (Passover/Unleavened Bread, Weeks/Shavuot, Tabernacles/Sukkot) brought enormous crowds to the city; the population multiplication of restored Israel is compared to the scale of these festival gatherings. The restored land will be filled with people as densely as Jerusalem during the great festivals.
